infobox.value: ([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *Türkiye Millî Basketbol Takımı*), recognized as **Türkiye** by [FIBA](/wiki/FIBA "FIBA"), represents [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") in international [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"). They are governed by the [Turkish Basketball Federation](/wiki/Turkish_Basketball_Federation "Turkish Basketball Federation"). Their nickname is the *12 Dev Adam*, meaning 12 Giant Men.

The **Turkey men's national basketball team** ([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *Türkiye Millî Basketbol Takımı*), recognized as **Türkiye** by [FIBA](/wiki/FIBA "FIBA"), represents [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") in international [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"). They are governed by the [Turkish Basketball Federation](/wiki/Turkish_Basketball_Federation "Turkish Basketball Federation"). Their nickname is the *12 Dev Adam*, meaning 12 Giant Men.

Turkey has competed at every major international basketball tournament. Their greatest achievements are winning two silver medals at the [EuroBasket](/wiki/EuroBasket "EuroBasket"), one on home soil in [2001](/wiki/EuroBasket_2001 "EuroBasket 2001"), and another in [2025](/wiki/EuroBasket_2025 "EuroBasket 2025"). They have also won silver at the [FIBA World Cup](/wiki/FIBA_World_Cup "FIBA World Cup"), as hosts in [2010](/wiki/2010_FIBA_World_Championship "2010 FIBA World Championship"). Turkey won two gold ([1987](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1987_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 1987 Mediterranean Games"), [2013](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2013_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 2013 Mediterranean Games")), one silver ([1971](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1971_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 1971 Mediterranean Games")), and three bronze ([1967](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1967_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 1967 Mediterranean Games"), [1983](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1983_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 1983 Mediterranean Games"), [2009](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2009_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 2009 Mediterranean Games")) medals at the [Mediterranean Games](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the Mediterranean Games"). They also won a gold medal at the [1981 Balkan Championship](/wiki/Balkan_Basketball_Championship "Balkan Basketball Championship").

Currently, Turkey stands 11th in the [FIBA World Ranking](/wiki/FIBA_Men%27s_World_Ranking "FIBA Men's World Ranking").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_men%27s_national_basketball_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Turkey_greece_1936.jpg/250px-Turkey_greece_1936.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turkey_greece_1936.jpg)

Turkey against Greece in 1936.

[Ahmet Robenson](/wiki/Ahmet_Robenson "Ahmet Robenson") was known as being the first organizer of basketball in [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey").[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 1936, Turkey played its first ever basketball match against [Greece](/wiki/Greece_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Greece men's national basketball team"), winning 49–12.

For many years basketball was a little-known sport in Turkey, but when the American drama [The White Shadow](/wiki/The_White_Shadow_(TV_series) "The White Shadow (TV series)") started airing in the country, it gained popularity. However, the national team at the time lacked experience and could not win any international tournaments until the 1980s, when Turkey won the gold medal at the [1981 Balkan Championship](/wiki/Balkan_Basketball_Championship "Balkan Basketball Championship") and the [1987 Mediterranean Games](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1987_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 1987 Mediterranean Games").

At the club level, [Efes Pilsen](/wiki/Anadolu_Efes_S.K. "Anadolu Efes S.K.") was the first Turkish club to win a European Cup in any team sport, the [1995–96 FIBA Korać Cup](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_FIBA_Kora%C4%87_Cup "1995–96 FIBA Korać Cup"). Since then, basketball in Turkey has grown immensely, as the national team began to play a major role in international tournaments. Turkey won the silver medal at both [EuroBasket 2001](/wiki/EuroBasket_2001 "EuroBasket 2001"), and the [2010 FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIBA_World_Championship "2010 FIBA World Championship").

### 1930s–1940s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_men%27s_national_basketball_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1930s–1940s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Turkey_national_basketball_team_in_1946-1947.jpg/250px-Turkey_national_basketball_team_in_1946-1947.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turkey_national_basketball_team_in_1946-1947.jpg)

Turkey national basketball team squad in 1946.

Turkey’s debut at international tournaments, came with the [1936 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1936_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 1936 Summer Olympics"), where they recorded two defeats against [Chile](/wiki/Chile_national_basketball_team "Chile national basketball team") and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_national_basketball_team "Egypt national basketball team").

Turkey's [European Championship](/wiki/EuroBasket "EuroBasket") debut came at the [EuroBasket 1949](/wiki/EuroBasket_1949 "EuroBasket 1949"). The Turks split their six matches in the seven-team round robin tournament, finishing with three wins and three losses for fourth place.[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Hüseyin Öztürk](/wiki/H%C3%BCseyin_%C3%96zt%C3%BCrk "Hüseyin Öztürk"), who was the scoring leader of the tournament, won the [MVP award](/wiki/FIBA_EuroBasket_MVP "FIBA EuroBasket MVP").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### 1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_men%27s_national_basketball_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1950s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Yalcin_Granit.jpg/250px-Yalcin_Granit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yalcin_Granit.jpg)

[Yalçın Granit](/wiki/Yal%C3%A7%C4%B1n_Granit "Yalçın Granit"), leading player during the 1950s, and the first Turkish basketballer to play in a European team.

Turkey competed again at the [EuroBasket 1951](/wiki/EuroBasket_1951 "EuroBasket 1951") in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"). Their only loss in the preliminary round was to the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Soviet Union men's national basketball team") as Turkey earned the second-rank spot with a 3–1 record. Their single loss to [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Bulgaria men's national basketball team") in the semi-final round, however, was enough to bump them from championship contention as they came out on the bottom of a three-way tie with a 2–1 record. They won their classification 5–8 game, but lost the 5/6 game to [Italy](/wiki/Italy_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Italy men's national basketball team").

Turkey returned after missing 1953's edition to the [EuroBasket 1955](/wiki/EuroBasket_1955 "EuroBasket 1955") in [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"). They went 1–2 in their preliminary round group, taking third in the pool and moving to classification play. There they lost only to [France](/wiki/France_men%27s_national_basketball_team "France men's national basketball team") on their way to a 3–1 record in classification round 1. They lost their 9–12 semi-final by 1 point to [Finland](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Finland men's national basketball team"), but defeated [England](/wiki/England_men%27s_national_basketball_team "England men's national basketball team") 77–54 in the next game to take 11th place of the 18 team tournament.

Turkey appeared again at the [EuroBasket 1957](/wiki/EuroBasket_1957 "EuroBasket 1957") in [Sofia](/wiki/Sofia "Sofia"). Losing to the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Soviet Union men's national basketball team") and [Poland](/wiki/Poland_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Poland men's national basketball team") in the preliminary round, Turkey took third in the group to be sent to the classification pool. They defeated each of the other seven teams in the classification round in order to take 9th place of the 16 teams. Turkey hosted [EuroBasket 1959](/wiki/EuroBasket_1959 "EuroBasket 1959"), but displayed a poor performance during the tournament finishing 12th.

### 1960s–1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_men%27s_national_basketball_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1960s–1980s")]

The 1960s, 1970s and 1980s were in general a barren period for the Turkish national basketball team. They were, however, successful at the [Mediterranean Games](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the Mediterranean Games"), winning two bronze medals in [1967](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1967_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 1967 Mediterranean Games") and [1983](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1983_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 1983 Mediterranean Games"), one silver medal in [1971](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1971_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 1971 Mediterranean Games"), and one gold medal in [1987](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1987_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 1987 Mediterranean Games"). Turkey also won the gold medal at the [1981 Balkan Championship](/wiki/Balkan_Basketball_Championship "Balkan Basketball Championship"). The team was led by notable coaches like Samim Göreç and Mehmet Baturalp in the 1960s and 1970s, and by [Aydan Siyavuş](/wiki/Aydan_Siyavu%C5%9F "Aydan Siyavuş") during the Balkan and Mediterranean triumphs of the 1980s, which marked the dawn of a successful new era in Turkish basketball (especially in European club competitions) starting from the late 1980s and early 1990s. [Efe Aydan](/wiki/Efe_Aydan "Efe Aydan") and [Erman Kunter](/wiki/Erman_Kunter "Erman Kunter") were among the notable players of this period in Turkish basketball. Erman Kunter, who still holds a number of all-time records in the [Turkish Basketball League](/wiki/Turkish_Basketball_League "Turkish Basketball League") as a player, later became a successful coach in the Turkish and French basketball leagues, and led the Turkish national team at the [EuroBasket 1999](/wiki/EuroBasket_1999 "EuroBasket 1999").

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_men%27s_national_basketball_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990s")]

Turkey appeared again at the [EuroBasket 1993](/wiki/EuroBasket_1993 "EuroBasket 1993") after 12 years of absence, but finished 11th among 16 teams. Turkey finished 13th among 14 teams at the [EuroBasket 1995](/wiki/EuroBasket_1995 "EuroBasket 1995"), 8th among 16 teams at the [EuroBasket 1997](/wiki/EuroBasket_1997 "EuroBasket 1997"), and again 8th among 16 teams at the [EuroBasket 1999](/wiki/EuroBasket_1999 "EuroBasket 1999").

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_men%27s_national_basketball_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Turkey_74-76_Greece%2C_EuroBasket.jpg/250px-Turkey_74-76_Greece%2C_EuroBasket.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turkey_74-76_Greece,_EuroBasket.jpg)

Turkey against Greece during the [EuroBasket 2009](/wiki/EuroBasket_2009 "EuroBasket 2009") in Poland.

As the host country of the [EuroBasket 2001](/wiki/EuroBasket_2001 "EuroBasket 2001"), the Turkish national basketball team, led by coach [Aydın Örs](/wiki/Ayd%C4%B1n_%C3%96rs "Aydın Örs"), reached the EuroBasket final for the first time in their history. Turkey defeated [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Croatia men's national basketball team") in the quarter-finals and [Germany](/wiki/Germany_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Germany men's national basketball team") in the semis, before their showdown with [FR Yugoslavia](/wiki/Serbia_and_Montenegro "Serbia and Montenegro") in the final. Turkey would ultimately lose the game {69–78}, finishing the Euros with the silver medal.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Furthermore, Turkish stars [İbrahim Kutluay](/wiki/%C4%B0brahim_Kutluay "İbrahim Kutluay") scored 19 points in the final game, while [Hidayet Türkoğlu](/wiki/Hedo_T%C3%BCrko%C4%9Flu "Hedo Türkoğlu") was named to the [All-Tournament](/wiki/FIBA_EuroBasket_All-Tournament_Team "FIBA EuroBasket All-Tournament Team") team. The now-infamous march of the team, 12 Dev Adam by [Athena](/wiki/Athena_(band) "Athena (band)") was published the same year.

Due to their surprising finish at EuroBasket 2001, Turkey qualified for their first appearance to the [FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIBA_World_Championship "2002 FIBA World Championship") in 2002. Turkey, however, would accumulate just four wins against four defeats at the World Cup to finish the tournament in ninth.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

At the [EuroBasket 2003](/wiki/EuroBasket_2003 "EuroBasket 2003"), Turkey reached the second round where they got eliminated by Serbia and Montenegro.

Turkey qualified for the [EuroBasket 2005](/wiki/EuroBasket_2005 "EuroBasket 2005") held in [Serbia and Montenegro](/wiki/Serbia_and_Montenegro "Serbia and Montenegro"), but lost to [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Lithuania men's national basketball team") (87–75) and Croatia (80–67), defeating only [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Bulgaria men's national basketball team") (94–89) in the preliminary round. This win brought Turkey to the knock-out stage, where [Germany](/wiki/Germany_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Germany men's national basketball team") eliminated the team 66–57. Turkey ended the tournament with a 9–12 rank.

Turkey was awarded one of the four [wild cards](/wiki/Wild_card_(sports) "Wild card (sports)") by [FIBA](/wiki/FIBA "FIBA") for the qualification to the [2006 FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIBA_World_Championship "2006 FIBA World Championship") in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Basketball_World_Cup_2010_Turkey2.jpg/250px-Basketball_World_Cup_2010_Turkey2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Basketball_World_Cup_2010_Turkey2.jpg)

Hedo Türkoğlu, iconic player of Turkey during the 2000s.

The Turkish squad completed the preliminary round in [Group C](/wiki/2006_FIBA_World_Championship#Group_C_(Hamamatsu) "2006 FIBA World Championship") in second place behind the [Greek team](/wiki/Greece_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Greece men's national basketball team"), and was entitled to play in the [Round of 16](/wiki/2006_FIBA_World_Championship#Knockout_stage "2006 FIBA World Championship"). There the team defeated [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Slovenia men's national basketball team") 90–84, to advance into the quarter-finals of the World Cup for the first time in their history. However, In the quarter-finals, Turkey lost to [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Argentina men's national basketball team"), and was relegated to the classification round. There, Turkey would go on to defeat Lithuania once again, before losing to [France](/wiki/France_men%27s_national_basketball_team "France men's national basketball team") to finish the tournament in sixth place.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

After their surprising results at the tournament, a new nickname, *12 Cesur Yürek* (12 Brave Hearts) was proposed after the young and inexperienced teams' fearless performance at the 2006 FIBA World Cup, despite not having star players Hidayet Türkoğlu, [Mehmet Okur](/wiki/Mehmet_Okur "Mehmet Okur"), [Mirsad Türkcan](/wiki/Mirsad_T%C3%BCrkcan "Mirsad Türkcan"), [Kerem Tunçeri](/wiki/Kerem_Tun%C3%A7eri "Kerem Tunçeri") and [Hüseyin Beşok](/wiki/H%C3%BCseyin_Be%C5%9Fok "Hüseyin Beşok").

At the [EuroBasket 2007](/wiki/EuroBasket_2007 "EuroBasket 2007"), Turkey played in Group C with [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Lithuania men's national basketball team"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Germany men's national basketball team") and the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Czech Republic men's national basketball team"). Turkey began by losing 86–69 to Lithuania and was then blown out 79–49 by Germany. The team ended group play with an 80–72 win over fellow bottom-of-the-group team Czech Republic. With that win Turkey advanced to the next round, although the team lost all of its games (66–51 to Slovenia, 84–75 to Italy and 85–64 to France) in that phase. The three results eliminated Turkey from the EuroBasket 2007 with an (1–5) record and an 11th-place finish.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

At the [EuroBasket 2009](/wiki/EuroBasket_2009 "EuroBasket 2009"), Turkey played in Group D with hosts [Poland](/wiki/Poland_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Poland men's national basketball team"), Lithuania and [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Bulgaria men's national basketball team"). The Group D matches took place in [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"), Poland. Turkey won all three of its group matches and qualified for the second round.

Turkey enjoyed a strong start in the second round, as they won their first match against World Champions [Spain](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Spain men's national basketball team") 63–60; when [Ömer Aşık](/wiki/%C3%96mer_A%C5%9F%C4%B1k "Ömer Aşık") and [Ersan İlyasova](/wiki/Ersan_%C4%B0lyasova "Ersan İlyasova") rejected a last-second shot by [Sergio Llull](/wiki/Sergio_Llull "Sergio Llull"). Turkey then beat Serbia in overtime. Turkey's winning streak came to an end against Slovenia, when, despite a 19-point comeback, [Engin Atsür](/wiki/Engin_Ats%C3%BCr "Engin Atsür") missed a desperation three pointer in a 69–67 loss.

In the knockout stage, Turkey were against Greece and lost a close game in overtime, finishing the tournament 8th.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Yet, in the same year, Turkey won the bronze medal at the [2009 Mediterranean Games](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2009_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 2009 Mediterranean Games").

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_men%27s_national_basketball_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/La_selecci%C3%B3n_turca_de_baloncesto_tras_recibir_la_medalla_de_plata.jpg/250px-La_selecci%C3%B3n_turca_de_baloncesto_tras_recibir_la_medalla_de_plata.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_selecci%C3%B3n_turca_de_baloncesto_tras_recibir_la_medalla_de_plata.jpg)

Turkey hosted the [2010 FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIBA_World_Championship "2010 FIBA World Championship") and won the silver medal, falling to the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_basketball_team "United States men's national basketball team") in the final.

As the host country, Turkey, led by coach [Bogdan Tanjević](/wiki/Bogdan_Tanjevi%C4%87 "Bogdan Tanjević"), automatically qualified for the [2010 FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIBA_World_Championship "2010 FIBA World Championship"). Turkey topped their group, winning all of their games against the [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Ivory Coast men's national basketball team"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Russia men's national basketball team"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Greece men's national basketball team"), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Puerto Rico men's national basketball team") and [China](/wiki/China_men%27s_national_basketball_team "China men's national basketball team"). In the knockout stages Turkey managed to defeat [France](/wiki/France_men%27s_national_basketball_team "France men's national basketball team"), [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Slovenia men's national basketball team") and [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Serbia men's national basketball team") in a thrilling semi-final match which Turkey won 83–82.

The national team enjoyed their greatest success to date on the international stage going undefeated in group play and progressing to the final, where they eventually lost to the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_basketball_team "United States men's national basketball team") to come away with the silver medal.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Each player was awarded a prize of 1,500,000 TL (approximately $1,000,000 in September 2010) from the Turkish government for being the runner-up in the competition.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[Orhun Ene](/wiki/Orhun_Ene "Orhun Ene"), who was the assistant coach of Turkey next to Tanjević and had a successful career as a player, was appointed the head coach to lead Turkey during the [EuroBasket 2011](/wiki/EuroBasket_2011 "EuroBasket 2011"). The team had a disappointing tournament, as they were eliminated in the second group phase after losing three straight games. Turkey and Serbia were the only teams that managed to defeat the eventual EuroBasket 2011 champions [Spain](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Spain men's national basketball team") during the tournament.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Two years later, Turkey won their second gold medal at the [2013 Mediterranean Games](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2013_Mediterranean_Games "Basketball at the 2013 Mediterranean Games") by defeating [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Serbia men's national basketball team") in the final. A few months later at the [EuroBasket 2013](/wiki/EuroBasket_2013 "EuroBasket 2013"), Turkey managed to defeat only [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Sweden men's national basketball team"), before finishing the competition with a (1–4) record to fail making it out of the group stage.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Ergin_Ataman_20250218_%281%29.jpg/250px-Ergin_Ataman_20250218_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ergin_Ataman_20250218_(1).jpg)

[Ergin Ataman](/wiki/Ergin_Ataman "Ergin Ataman") coached the national team at the [2014 FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "2014 FIBA Basketball World Cup"), and was chosen as the best coach of [EuroBasket 2025](/wiki/EuroBasket_2025 "EuroBasket 2025")

On 22 March 2014, the [Turkish Basketball Federation](/wiki/Turkish_Basketball_Federation "Turkish Basketball Federation") announced that [Ergin Ataman](/wiki/Ergin_Ataman "Ergin Ataman") would lead Turkey during the [2014 FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "2014 FIBA Basketball World Cup"), [EuroBasket 2015](/wiki/EuroBasket_2015 "EuroBasket 2015") and the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics").[[16]](#cite_note-16) After receiving a [wild card](/wiki/Wild_card_(sports) "Wild card (sports)") bid, Turkey qualified for the 2014 FIBA World Cup.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The team was slotted into [Group C](/wiki/2014_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup_Group_C "2014 FIBA Basketball World Cup Group C"), to begin their tournament run. Turkey would eventually accumulate a (3–2) record during the group phase to advance into the knockout rounds. In the Round of 16, Turkey earned a hard-fought victory over [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Australia men's national basketball team") 65–64. In the quarter-finals, Turkey's tournament appearance would come to an end as the national team fell to [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Lithuania men's national basketball team") 73–61.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

At [EuroBasket 2015](/wiki/EuroBasket_2015 "EuroBasket 2015"), Turkey arrived on to the continental stage with confidence, after their solid play at the 2014 FIBA World Cup. Although the team would suffer a setback after getting past the group stage, as they were thoroughly outplayed in their Round of 16 match against [France](/wiki/France_men%27s_national_basketball_team "France men's national basketball team") 76–53.[[19]](#cite_note-19) After the disappointment for Turkey in 2015, head coach Ergin Ataman still had the responsibility to guide Turkey to the [2016 Olympic Tournament](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Basketball at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament"). The national team would once again have a let down, coming away with a (1–2) record during [qualifying](/wiki/2016_FIBA_World_Olympic_Qualifying_Tournament_%E2%80%93_Manila "2016 FIBA World Olympic Qualifying Tournament – Manila") and being eliminated.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

On 19 January 2017, [Ufuk Sarıca](/wiki/Ufuk_Sar%C4%B1ca "Ufuk Sarıca") succeeded Ergin Ataman as Turkey's new head coach.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Sarıca was among the star players of [Efes Pilsen](/wiki/Anadolu_Efes_S.K. "Anadolu Efes S.K.") in the 1990s, winning the [1995–96 FIBA Korać Cup](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_FIBA_Kora%C4%87_Cup "1995–96 FIBA Korać Cup") and reaching the final of the [1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_FIBA_European_Cup "1992–93 FIBA European Cup") [FIBA Saporta Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Saporta_Cup "FIBA Saporta Cup") with the Istanbul-based club under head coach [Aydın Örs](/wiki/Ayd%C4%B1n_%C3%96rs "Aydın Örs").

Turkey was named one of four co-hosts for the [EuroBasket 2017](/wiki/EuroBasket_2017 "EuroBasket 2017").[[22]](#cite_note-22) However, the national team put up a near identical performance as they did at the [2015 edition](/wiki/EuroBasket_2015 "EuroBasket 2015") Turkey was knocked out in the Round of 16 both times, although winning one fewer game in 2017.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In qualification for Turkey to make it to the [2019 FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2019_FIBA_World_Cup "2019 FIBA World Cup"), the national team posted an (8–4) record during [European Qualifiers](/wiki/2019_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup_qualification_(Europe) "2019 FIBA Basketball World Cup qualification (Europe)") to make it to their fifth consecutive World Cup finals appearance.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Entering the 2019 FIBA World Cup, Turkey was placed into [Group E](/wiki/2019_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup_Group_E "2019 FIBA Basketball World Cup Group E") where they defeated [Japan](/wiki/Japan_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Japan men's national basketball team") in their first match 86–67.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In their next game the national team were up against the United States. In the highly competitive back and forth battle between the two sides that needed overtime to decide a winner, it was the Americans who pulled out the narrow 93–92 victory. After the demoralizing loss, Turkey would go on to lose their final match of the preliminary phase 76–91 to the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Czech Republic men's national basketball team"), which relegated the team to the classification round to finish out the tournament.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_men%27s_national_basketball_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2020s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Alperen_%C5%9Eeng%C3%BCn_23_T%C3%BCrkiye_20250823_%281%29.jpg/250px-Alperen_%C5%9Eeng%C3%BCn_23_T%C3%BCrkiye_20250823_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alperen_%C5%9Eeng%C3%BCn_23_T%C3%BCrkiye_20250823_(1).jpg)

[Alperen Şengün](/wiki/Alperen_%C5%9Eeng%C3%BCn "Alperen Şengün"), an [NBA All-Star](/wiki/NBA_All-Star "NBA All-Star"), was a key player in both the 2022 and 2025 tournaments.

[Orhun Ene](/wiki/Orhun_Ene "Orhun Ene") was appointed head coach of Turkey for a second term on 23 November 2020. After Turkey failed to qualify for the [2023 FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2023_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "2023 FIBA Basketball World Cup"), Ene resigned and was replaced by [Ergin Ataman](/wiki/Ergin_Ataman "Ergin Ataman").

Under coach Ergin Ataman, and players like [Cedi Osman](/wiki/Cedi_Osman "Cedi Osman"), [Furkan Korkmaz](/wiki/Furkan_Korkmaz "Furkan Korkmaz"), [Shane Larkin](/wiki/Shane_Larkin "Shane Larkin"), and [Alperen Şengün](/wiki/Alperen_%C5%9Eeng%C3%BCn "Alperen Şengün"), Turkey reached the knockout stage of the [EuroBasket 2022](/wiki/EuroBasket_2022 "EuroBasket 2022"), where they were eliminated by [France](/wiki/France_national_basketball_team "France national basketball team").

At the [EuroBasket 2025](/wiki/EuroBasket_2025 "EuroBasket 2025"), Turkey reached the final for the first time since 24 years and settled for the silver medal. Alperen Şengün recorded 28 points in an 83–88 loss against [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_basketball_team "Germany national basketball team").
